text:
Admission Date:  2004-12-18              Discharge Date:   1990-11-22

Date of Birth:  2018-09-29             Sex:   M

Service: NEUROSURGERY

Allergies:
No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions

Attending:Stephanie
Chief Complaint:
Dizziness / L parietal IPH / AVM

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
1986-02-22: Cerebral angiogram with embolization


History of Present Illness:
This is a 47 year old gentleman with a tobacco history who
complained of  dizziness at home this afternoon while in
bathroom. He called EMS himself and was taken to Vibra Hospital Of Western Mass Central Campus. At the OSH he reportedly experienced expressive
aphasia. Head CT revealed left parietal hemorrhage. Transferred
to Providence Kodiak Island Medical Center and Neurosurgery consulted for evaluation.


Past Medical History:
None reported by patient

Social History:
works as a cleaner. Life long tobacco use. social Etoh. no drugs
spanish speaking


Family History:
unknown

Physical Exam:
On Admissio:
O: T: 97.8  BP: 154/93  HR:91    R18   O2Sats 100% RA
Gen: laying on stretcher, mildly restless
HEENT: Pupils: PERRL    EOMs intact
Neck: Supple.
Lungs: CTA bilaterally.
Cardiac: RRR.
Abd: Soft, NT
Extrem: Warm and well-perfused.
Neuro:
Mental status: Awake and alert, cooperative with exam, normal
affect.
Orientation: Oriented to person, place, and day, not year(2003).
Language: Spanish Speaking. Speech fluent per interpreter but
appears somewhat garbled per this examiner.

Cranial Nerves:
I: Not tested
II: Pupils equally round and reactive to light, 4 to 3
mm bilaterally.
III, IV, VI: Extraocular movements intact bilaterally without
nystagmus.
V, VII: Facial strength and sensation intact and symmetric.
VIII: Hearing intact to voice.
IX, X: Palatal elevation symmetrical.
Toni: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius normal bilaterally.
XII: Tongue midline without fasciculations.

Motor: Normal bulk and tone bilaterally. No abnormal movements,
tremors. Strength full power 09-19 throughout. No pronator drift

Sensation: Intact to light touch, no numbness or tingling

At discharge: pleasant and cooperative, improved expressive
aphasia, slight dysphasia.  PERRL, face symmetric, tongue
protrudes at midline.  Motor is full bilaterally, sensory intact
to light touch.  Reflex 2+ throughout, no hyperreflexia or upper
motor signs.

Pertinent Results:
CT TORSO 2001-05-31:
IMPRESSION: No evidence of intrathoracic or abdominal
malignancy.

MRI brain 2004-12-18:
FINDINGS:
An area of T2 hyperintensity and T1 hypointensity is noted in
the left
parietal lobe which appears hypointense on gradient echo images
suggestive of intraparenchymal hemorrhage. There is associated
mild perilesional edema with mass effect on the left lateral
ventricle. Multiple serpiginous flow voids are noted along the
superior and posterior aspect of the hemorrhage with a large
area of flow void superiorly measuring 3.1 x 2.1 cm which likely
represents a venous varix. The multiple serpiginous flow voids
likely represent nidus of the arteriovenous malformation. The
nidus measures approximately 2.9 x 3.7 x 3.3 cm in craniocaudad,
AP and transverse dimensions. A large draining vein is noted
extending from the venous varix into the superior sagittal
sinus. On post-contrast images there is heterogeneous
enhancement of the nidus and of the venous varix.

Brainstem and cerebellum appear normal. The major intracranial
flow voids are maintained.

IMPRESSION:
Large intraparenchymal hemorrhage in left parietal lobe with
associated
multiple serpiginous flow voids posterosuperior to the
hemorrhage which
represents nidus of arteriovenous malformation. A large venous
varix is noted superior to the nidus. A draining vein is noted
from the venous varix draining into the superior sagittal sinus.


Head CT 2004-12-18:
IMPRESSION:
1. Acute intraparenchymal hemorrhage with internal areas of
hypoattenuation suggest ongoing hemorrhage. No interval increase
in size of the lesion is noted, but there has been worsening of
perilesional edema with mild increase in the rightward midline
shift and effacement of the left lateral ventricle.
2. Two hyperattenuating lesions were compared with MR findings
that
demonstrate multiple tubular structures with flow voids in the
lesions
suggesting large arteriovenous malformation.
3. Opacification of ethmoidal sinuses and mild concentric
thickening of right sphenoidal sinus consistent with chronic
sinusitis.

Head CTA 2004-12-18:
1. Acute intraparenchymal hemorrhage in the left corona radiata,
which is
unchanged since the prior study.
2. Arteriovenous malformation noted posterosuperior to the
hemorrhage with a
large venous varix. The main arterial supply of the
arteriovenous
malformation is from the left middle cerebral artery branches.
The drainage
of the arteriovenous malformation is into the superior sagittal
and left
sigmoid sinuses. There is some drainage into the deep venous
system.
3. No evidence of stenosis, occlusion, or aneurysm in the
arteries of head.

CT Head 09-04
1. Acute intraparenchymal hemorrhage in the left corona radiata,
which is
unchanged since the prior study. No evidence of new hemorrhage.

2. Streak artefact is noted in the nidus of arteriovenous
malformation due to embolization and that region is difficult to
assess due to the artefact.

10-05 - UE U/S -
1. No evidence of DVT in the right upper extremity.
2. Nearly occlusive thrombus within the right cephalic vein, as
described
above.




Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. Phillips was admitted to Providence Kodiak Island Medical Center on 1976-03-24. He was loaded with
Dilantin at the OSH and this was continued. He was on Q4 hr
neuro checks. There was a concern for an underlying mass. He had
a CT Torso that showed no malignancy. An MRI brain was performed
on 12-20 which showed no lesion but a large L parietal AVM. He
appeared more confused on 12-20 and a repeat CT head showed an
increase in surrounding edema and shift. He was transferred to
the ICU for further monitoring. A head CTA was done for
treatment planning. He was made NPO and was taken to INR suite
for a cerebral angio for embolization on 09-04.

In angio, AVM was successfully embolized with onyx. Post angio
exam was stable. His dilantin level was corrected to be 9.9, he
recieved a 500mg bolus. On 01-10, his dilantin level was now
theraputic and exam remained stable. His sbp was liberalized to
90-140. On 02-22, patient was transferred to the floor and sbp
liberalized to 160.

On 10-05, his RUE was erythematous and edematous. A RUE
ultrasound was performed which revealed a superfical cephalic
thrombosis. It was determined that he would return in 1 month
for CK for further treatment of his AVM.

Now DOD, he is afebrile, VSS, and neuro stable.  He is
tolerating POs and pain is well-controlled.  He was discharged
home with outpatient speech therapy.

Medications on Admission:
None

Discharge Medications:
1. oxycodone-acetaminophen 5-325 mg Tablet Sig: 1-2 Tablets PO
Q4H (every 4 hours) as needed for pain.
Disp:*40 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
2. phenytoin sodium extended 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule
PO TID (3 times a day).
Disp:*90 Capsule(s)* Refills:*2*
3. docusate sodium 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO BID (2
times a day).
Disp:*60 Capsule(s)* Refills:*0*
4. senna 8.6 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).
Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
5. acetaminophen 325 mg Tablet Sig: 1-2 Tablets PO Q6H (every 6
hours) as needed for pain.


Discharge Disposition:
Home

Discharge Diagnosis:
L parietal IPH
L parietal AVM
Cerebral edema with compression
Expressive aphasia
Superficial cephalic thrombosis


Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker
or cane).


Discharge Instructions:
Instructions for Follow up
??????	Take your pain medicine as prescribed if needed. You do not
need to take it if you do not have pain.
??????	Exercise should be limited to walking; no lifting >10lbs,
straining, or excessive bending.
??????	Increase your intake of fluids and fiber, as narcotic pain
medicine can cause constipation. We generally recommend taking
an over the counter stool softener, such as Docusate (Colace)
while taking narcotic pain medication.
??????	DO not take ASA or anticoagulant until cleared by Neurosurgery
??????	***You have been prescribed Dilantin (Phenytoin), take it as
prescribed and follow up with laboratory blood drawing in two
weeks.  This can be drawn at your PCP??????s office, but please have
the results faxed to 358-436-8309.
??????	Do not drive until your follow up appointment.


Followup Instructions:
Our office will contact you with an appointment to return for a
cerebral angiogram. We will coordinate with Dr Carlson for
CyberKnife (radiation) planning.

Please follow up with your PCP for repeat [**Name9 (PRE) 167**] Upper Extremitiy
Dopplers to further evaluate the superfical cephalic clot seen
on ultrasound while in hospital. This can be repeated in 07-20
weeks after discharge.



Completed by:1990-11-22
